content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Any films that isn’t quite a kids’ movie, but isn’t fully an adult one either?

**Score:** 208
**Body:** 
I'm looking for movies that blur the line between children and adult media


Obviously, they can have an official rating  that's objectively identifiable by the rating system

But when you actually watch these films, it's themes, jokes, humour, and subject matter makes you think to yourself...

 "Is this actually a kids film?" Or "Is this actually made for adults?"

Those are the types of films I'm looking for. And I  hope to discover some pretty cool ones from y'all 

I can't wait to see what y'all got!



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Coraline (2009)
2. Who framed Roger rabbit
3. I’d say a lot of Studio Ghibli’s output
4. The Princess Bride
5. Pee-wee’s Big Adventure
6. 9
7. Big
8. School of Rock
9. Also The Dark Crystal, The Labyrinth and Mirrormask. All Jim Henson Creature Shop stuff, especially the older stuff blur the lines.
10. Cruella. It feels like it’s made for kids, but I can see it being boring for younger ones. It’s an excellent fun film!

Mirrormask. Visually stunning and quite dark at times.

The Fall. Has a childlike perspective, but handles heavy themes. One of the most beautiful films I’ve seen.

## 2. Movies similar to "Bone Tomahawk" or "Apocalypto"

**Score:** 40
**Body:** I'm looking for movies with the same intense, gritty, and immersive style as *Bone Tomahawk* or *Apocalypto*. I love films that combine brutal realism with tense survival situations, historical or frontier settings, and strong, often silent storytelling. Any recommendations for films that capture that same raw, cinematic tension and visceral atmosphere?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Revenant (2015)
2. The Last of the Mohicans. This is a 5/5 must watch. Stacked cast, Michael Mann directed ting.
3. The movie 'Apostle' on Netflix, set in the year 1905, a guy finds out his sister is being held for ransom on an island populated by a religious cult, so he infiltrates the island and tries to blend in until he can find his sister
4. The Northman
5. The 13th Warrior. Almost 30 years old now so definitely not as gritty but for it's time I enjoyed it. Banderas is the lead but lots of relatively unknown actors that perform well.
6. The Proposition (2005).
7. Green Room. Just trust me.
8. The Road. It’s a hard watch, though
9. Prey
10. Ravenous

## 3. Fantastic Planet(1973)

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Any amazing throwbacks like fantastic planet anyone would recommend?! Love the sci-fi, weirdness, experimental imagery but also the amazing plot and conceptually rich message. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love Fantastic Planet! Not the same but the original Fritz Lang Metropolis is pretty amazing
  - Metropolis is such a trip, that whole silent era sci-fi aesthetic hits different when you're in the right mood for it
  - As soon as I saw Fritz, my mind went, "The Cat?"
2. The Snails 1966 (Les Escargots) by Rene Laloux

Wizards 1977
3. Gandahar

Zardoz
4. Journey To The Center Of The Earth! The old one!
  - Memory unlocked! This and First Men In The Moon (1964) were childhood staples for me and my brothers!
5. Scavengers Reign is a TV series and pretty new, but clearly very inspired by fantastic planet, and is super cool.
6. About half of Heavy Metal (1981)
7. The Day the Earth Stood Still (50s version) is a must-watch

The Twilight Zone episode 'To Serve Man' is great too

The original Planet of the Apes movie is worth a watch (1968)

I'd also highly recommend the radio series X-Minus 1 from the 50s.

## 4. I’m looking for suggestions for movies that subvert expectations by being strangely touching and wholesome

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I watched Lars and the Real Girl recently at my friend’s recommendation and I was surprised at how touching and sweet it really was. Does anyone have recommendations for more movies with that vibe? Movies that surprise you by being wholesome if that makes sense! Thanks in advance! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hunt for the Wilderpeople is peak this vibe, starts chaotic then hits you right in the feelings. Paddington 2 sounds like a joke pick but it’s genuinely warm and kind. Little Miss Sunshine sneaks up on you too, messy people being weirdly wholesome.
2. The Swedish film: A Man Called Ove (2015)
3. The lake house.  Two weeks notice.  Knotting hill.  You’ve got mail.  And also not “romcom” that makes sense for your parameters.   “Seeking a friend for the end of the world”.
  - Thank you so much! I have never heard of a few of these, can’t wait to watch 😄
4. Waitress was the first that came to mind.
5. Planes, Trains, & Automobiles (1987)

Big Daddy (1999)
6. 40-Year-Old Virgin
7. I don't know that they subverted expectations or were strange. But I think they were films that folks have forgotten or maybe never saw.

Untamed Heart

Punch Drunk Love

The Shape of Water

The Wrestler
8. Are you open to animated films? I think Mary and Max might have a similar vibe to what you’re looking for, but it’s a stop motion movie. It’s not rated but I’d put it at like a PG-13 maybe
9. An oldie but goodie:

Harold and Maude

## 5. Films like Seven (1995)?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I'm looking for movie recommendations about investigators or detectives solving crimes or a chain of murders. Strong action, constant tension, dark and thrilling stories, and lots of plot twists. Similar in tone to Se7en

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. * *Zodiac* (2007)
* *Memories of murder* (2003)
2. Cure (1997)
3. Mindhunter

It’s a Netflix series by David Fincher. 2 FBI agents in 1977 study the behaviors of serial killers by interviewing them and finding patterns to catch other active serial killers.
4. Gone baby gone is another (albeit private investigator). 

Mystic River as well.  

Both make you want to crawl into a fetal position.  All 3 great and critically acclaimed.  Multiple awards including Oscar’s for mystic river.

Bone Collector is great (Denzel and Angelina Jolie.  Double jeopardy (Tommy Lee jones and Ashley Judd).

All of Morgan freeman’s Alex cross movies.  None are as gut wrenching as the first two I mentioned like se7en if that’s what you are looking for but good detective movies.
5. Girl with the Dragon Tattoo
6. Along Came A Spider, The Bone Collector
7. Manhunter (1986), Longlegs.
8. The first time I saw Seven I thought it was terrible and one of the most unpleasant moviegoing experiences of my life.  **But**, I couldn't stop thinking about it for days, and a week later I went to see it for a second time and loved it (well, "loved" may not be quite the right word, but I thought it was brilliant).  For what it's worth, there has only been one other movie that produced in me this same reaction, the brilliant but *very* unsettling 1987 movie Angel Heart.  It's an experience for sure, and does meet your stated criteria.
9. Zodiac 
Mind hunter (tv series)
10. The Batman

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name Actor With Incredible Range

**Score:** 2780

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tilda Swinton. 
2. Gary Oldman. 
It's always Gary Oldman.
  - Actor with Range? It's Gary Oldman
Actor that's a chameleon? It's Gary Oldman 

Javier Bardem does his thing too though
3. Phillip Seymore Hoffman
4. Christian Bale deserves to on this list for sure. Dude has mad range
  - Specifically 120lbs to 200lbs
5. Willem Defoe
6. Michael Keaton
7. Michael Fassbender
8. Daniel Day Lewis. No contest.

## 2. Disneys John Carter (2012) is considered one of the biggest, if not the biggest, financial box office flops of all time. Would it have been a success if it were released in a different year with a less competitive box office lineup? I'm partway thru it, and curious why it failed.

**Score:** 338

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The marketing for this movie was atrocious. Even if you were always online or watching TV, you'd see almost nothing about this movie. And any clips or images you'd see would all just be "JOHN CARTER" and a ragged, caveman-looking John Carter. That's it. Even that trailer above shows you next to nothing more than what i've described except a second of an actual alien.

I'm not sure if they just half-assed it, or if they really thought people already knew who John Carter was AND would be so hyped to see him on the screen that ample marketing would be unnecessary. I've heard that it gets mentioned in marketing textbooks for like Intro to Marketing type courses.

I genuinely like the movie btw. His "last stand" scene interwoven with his return to his homestead flashback is a memorable and well-done scene for sure.
  - For some reason, everyone involved in making this movie thought that John Carter would have the same kind of recognition as LOTR and it would sell itself.
2. less focus on john more focus on mars could have saved this movie i think

nobody knew who john carter was so why bother
  - yes i’m saying a different title would have helped.
    - A terrible name for a movie.  Just so bland and boring.
    - I participated in a sort of test review for upcoming trailers and this was one of them. In that test trailer, it had the title "John Carter of Mars."
      - That title would have saved it I think. I've seen a few minutes of it and did not know Mars had anything to do with it till just now. Now I am curious enough to try and find out more.
3. I bet if they had called it "Warlord of Mars" it would have done way better. Kinda like the film 'Anthropoid' - who had aany idea what that would be about?
4. From what I understand, it’s mainly because the marketing for this movie was really bad
5. From what I understand, some idiots at Disney saw that the movie Mars Needs Moms had bombed the previous year and thought that having Mars in the title of John Carter might negatively impact it. So rather than calling it A Princess of Mars like the original story's title or a more modern John Carter of Mars or something like Edgar Rice Burrough's \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_, they just went with John Carter. Now this is a story that came out nearly a century before the movie did, so while it is fairly famous, it isn't as well known anymore and definitely wasn't in the general public's awareness, so by messing with the title and with horrid marketing, they cut the legs from underneath it.

## 3. What movie genre will never be popular again as it was before?

**Score:** 84

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Western. I highly doubt that we’ll ever return to the peak John Wayne days
  - It's a shame. There's been some damn good ones since the boom ended. 


Quick and the Dead, Hateful Eight, Magnificent Seven remake, Bone Tomahawk, True Grit remake. All good ones.
    - Tombstone
    - 3:10 to Yuma. True Grit has the be the best Western of all time.
      - Unforgiven might be the finest post-western western film of all time
2. 2025 was a big year for horror and not only that it was original storytelling which everyone can't stop complaining about. I think you mean "Franchises that won't reach their all time peak again"
3. Biblical epics
4. Comedies and spoofs peaked in the 2000s. A lot of them would be under fire today.
  - I'm excited to see how the Wayans Bros. do with Scary Movie 6. The whole cast is coming back. Dave Sheridan is coming back as Doofy lol. Don't know what kind of fire that's gonna cause
5. Romcoms… peaked in the 90s-00s

## 4. What is your favourite depiction of Death (Grim Reaper) in media?

**Score:** 71

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I am quite fond of Terry Pratchett's depiction of Death.
  - HELLO
    - *SQUEAK*
  - LORD, WHAT CAN THE HARVEST HOPE FOR, IF NOT FOR THE CARE OF THE REAPER MAN?
2. Grim from Billy and Mandy
3. Robert Redford in that Twighlight Zone episode and Brad Pitt in Meet Joe Black.
4. I do love William Sadler as the Grim Reaper in Bill & Ted’s Bogus Journey.

And Aubrey Plaza was pretty great as Death in Agatha All Along.
  - ***BEST 5 OUT OF 7...***
5. The Seventh Seal was perfect
6. Death from Supernatural.

## 5. The great flood (2026) I waited for so long for this movie and it was so bad I wish the main characters died

**Score:** 46
**Body:** Main girl every 10 seconds to the STRONG GUY who saved her and her son from drowning “let me hold him let me hold him let me hold him” babes u almost died twice and you want to hold your own child? And why are you crying about not being able to perform your work well when it’s the only reason you can escape from drowning? She doesn’t use her brain I swear, and she keeps making the strong guy pause just so she can ask questions like bitch get to safety FIRST ASK QUESTIONS LATER. Then her son wants to POOP in the middle of the water level rising LIKE JUST SHIT YOUR PANTS BUDDY. Ohhh my god and then the mother drowns AGAIN! Just die already! I would have a happier ending if they actually died! They deserve it! It’s like they want to die! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Okay so I didn’t finish the movie yet I’m still watching it and WILL BE COMMENTING EVERYTIME THE MOVIE RAGE BAITS ME
  - Damn it I am literally watching it right now…
2. I just wanted to watch for the apocalyptic special effects
3. She actually dies like 20k times.
4. She doesn’t even realize how privileged she is to have a seat on the airplane to survive and she acting like an IDIOT
5. I saw the movie being available today and immediatelly skipped it thinking it's trash but ur post made me wanna watch it regardless lmao.
Also I really think the movie should have ended at the 14 minute mark when the huge wave hits the building. Like how do you even survive that ffs
  - I thought I could watch this slop but ain't no way. I've decided to skip through it and peek at the "plot" and apocalyptic cgi, like I do with any other overpriced apocalyptic slop
6. Ok honestly I just skipped to the end F U C K this movie. I used to watch movies to the end even when they sucked but recently I’m like, I need to respect my time. Some of these movies are a waste of funding and labor it’s literally an INSULT to cinema.
7. Man it was so much worse than it should have been. I’ll never know how it ends. I bailed after like 40 minutes. I doubt I’ll regret it
8. AI SLOP CHAT GPT FILM. Korea please put the CAMERA DOWN PLEASE. On a good note I watched a VERY A+ FILM it’s called “NO OTHER CHOICE” next best thing since PARASITE

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Train Dreams (2025)

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Has anyone seen it yet?
The scenery of the movie is absolutely beautiful as well as the performances of the actors are really nice.
Legacy of the pain.
Loneliness, waiting, simplicity, getting older.
It is all shown in the movie.
We see Robert getting older which to me is stitched greatly to his job.
Cutting old trees while slowly growing into being forgotten.
All just atoms.
Yet even though a great fire came, trees stayed standing so even that death came, atoms get back to the earth, the consciousness with memories stayed.
The mind needs to categories everything, it needs to own thoughts and feelings.
The mind needs problems to solve from birth to death to fill the "gap", to fill the nothing that we are in order to stay alive from birth to death.
The mind doesn't need time, all is just happening, even memories through feelings are present.
I just wanted to say that the movie made me cry, that's all.
And also wanted to know your opinion 😌

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The book is one of my favorites and I have read it multiple times over the last 15 years or so, and I was unfortunately very let down by this adaptation. It seemed like every change they were making was in the interest of sanding down the rough edges of how messy and complicated people's lives are, and in a movie like this that seeks to portray the fullness of a man's life, that feels like a betrayal of the spirit of the thing. If you liked the movie I hope you check the book out some time, it is only a little over a hundred pages.
  - The book is also one of my favorites and I felt deeply underwhelmed by the film. The narration is exhausting and redundant, adding little to the imagery and holds the viewer’s hand at all times. There is no space for quiet reflection in the film, you’re on a guided tour of the man’s life at all times.
2. It’s a good movie, though I didn’t quite love it. It was a little too saccharine for my tastes and just a bit shallow in its rumination on life and death. And while absolutely beautiful, it kind of bothered me how many shots were at dusk or dawn. Aesthetically it just felt overused. That combined with how idealistic their life was portrayed prior to fire (despite the minor conflict of him having to leave every time it was logging season) made it feel like I was watching something artificially curated, like something designed for an Instagram post.
  - Agree it’s a good movie but also concur that technology nowadays probably makes so much easier to capture golden hour and crepuscular light
  - Shooting at dawn and dusk may be a matter of shooting without a permit? Which had to be done, on location, shooting "historically" about the period between 1880s and 1968 --
  - OMG came here to say the dusk/dawn shit was driving me nuts. It felt like they were filming on some alien planet where it's always sunset. Coupled with the excessive low angle constantly moving Terrance Malick-ish camera work, and it's all a big nope for me.
3. It was good, but it all felt a bit safe to me. The voiceover also spelled everything out, so I didn't feel as though I was being required to use my imagination that much. There were some great moments and it was beautifully shot, but I felt as though the sheer hardship of that lifestyle never really hit me. I was engaged with the subject matter, but I think it could have been wilder, more idiosyncratic and harder hitting than it was. 

If Debra Granik, Werner Herzog or Terence Malick had directed the exact same story, I think it would have been more what I was hoping for.
4. I absolutely loved this film. The pace, the acting, the cinematography. Alone with the melancholic feeling of aging and the wilderness.
I think I identify with Grainier which probably affects how much I like it. 
I picked up a copy of the novella and plan to read it over the holidays.
  - Niiiice, books are almost always a bit better so enjoy.
And yeah I think I started to tear up when Robert was at the fire watch tower with the lady, somewhere near the end
5. I watched it just the other night and found it really moving. Some people don’t like the narration but I found it helpful. It was hard to stomach the fire scenes, but the way they portrayed grief was so well done—kinda reminded me of Tree of Life. I don’t know if I’ll ever watch it again because it’s not exactly a comforting experience but it was beautiful and I am glad I took the time to see it. 

## 2. Enemy vs The Double: two kinds of loneliness, two different audiences

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Recently watched Enemy (2013) and The Double (2013), and I realized why one hit me deeply while the other didn’t, and I don’t think it’s about “understanding” the movies.

They’re about two completely different kinds of pain.

The Double is about structural loneliness.

Being invisible, replaceable, insignificant. The main character wants connection, stability, love, but the world doesn’t give it to him. His loneliness isn’t a flaw; it’s imposed. Love in that movie feels like recognition, like proof of existence (“I want to be real”). That’s why the film feels emotionally devastating rather than clever.

Enemy, on the other hand, is about self inflicted loneliness.

A man who already has stability and connection but sabotages it. His fear isn’t being erased, it’s being trapped. His loneliness comes from avoidance, not deprivation. The symbolism (doubles, spiders, keys) reflects fear of commitment and responsibility, not lack of connection.

What clicked for me is that these pains pull in opposite directions.

• The Double: “Let me in. See me.”

• Enemy: “Don’t trap me. Let me escape.”

Because of that, I don’t think these films resonate with the same people in the same way. If you’re experiencing structural loneliness, Enemy can feel frustrating or emotionally hollow, like watching someone waste what you’re striving for. If you relate to fear of commitment or self sabotage, The Double might feel distant or abstract.

I don’t think one film is smarter than the other. They just speak to different wounds.

Curious if others feel the same.

• Did one of these movies hit way harder than the other?

• Do you think people can truly relate to both at the same time, or do they tend to resonate with one depending on where they are in life?

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love these two movies as a pairing. They are essentially the same story, but they’re 

The Double is based on Fyodor Dostoevsky’s book by the same name, and boy howdy does that movie’s environment drip with Russian/Soviet influence.

The Enemy is based on a book called The Double that was a modern retelling of Dostoevsky’s book. 

I really liked both of them, two sides of the same coin.
2. I got more out of The Double on a comedic level. I liked the approach. Ayoade compared the novel (or at least the central idea of the novel) to an episode of Curb Your Enthusiasm. The absurd comes through more in The Double.

## 3. No Other Choice (2025) and South Korean involvement in the Vietnam War

**Score:** 23
**Body:** A huge part of South Korean industrialization process was from US money sent to SK for sending 350,000 troops to fight in the Vietnam war. I had no idea before watching this movie and it lead to me reading about this part of South Korean history - and that context of that puts so much of the movie into perspective. The "blowback" of Man-su's pointless (but prideful) "war" for money and all for maintain his family's standard of living.

I'm curious if someone can point me in the direction of more commentary on the perspective of south korean's massive involvement (and war crimes) in the Vietnam

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Looked into the topic, and it seems Korean films tend to focus more on the aftermath than on the action itself. I came across White Badge (1992), Sunny (2008), and Ode to My Father (2014), though I can’t really speak to their quality yet, would probably give them a watch sometime after No Other Choice. I’m on a similar topic now, but looking from a different angle, the French side. If you’d like to broaden the scope a bit, I’d recommend films by Pierre Schoendoerffer: The 317th Platoon (1965), Dien Bien Phu (1992), and the documentary The Anderson Platoon (1967).

## 4. Civil War prequels?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I don’t want to overshare a fully formed theory yet, but I’m curious whether others feel this resonance too.

Watching Civil War, I was struck by how intentionally it avoids explaining origins—the film refuses to tell us why the war happened. Later, as I watched One Battle After Another and Eddington, it started to feel like a coherent way of reading the films together, not by plot or characters, but by stages of national collapse. In a sense, the latter two films increasingly fill in the gaps that Civil War leaves intentionally blank.

I’ve also been using AI to help me organize my thoughts and phrase this clearly—mostly to clarify what I’m trying to say, not to generate the idea itself.

Eddington feels like a society where reality and legitimacy are fracturing.
One Battle After Another feels like violence has become normalized and partially institutionalized.
Civil War feels like the endpoint, where ideology no longer matters and the war simply exists.

To be clear: I’m not claiming shared intent or a literal shared universe—there’s no evidence for that. Still, if someone told me these films were meant to exist in the same world, I’d be genuinely mind-blown and probably say, “I knew it all along.”

I’m mostly interested in whether, read together, these films feel like they’re circling the same historical moment from different distances—or whether I’m projecting structure where there isn’t any.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Punishment Park (1971). 

This pseudo-documentary style film depicts National Guardsmen and police pursuing members of a counterculture group (often described as hippies and anti-war activists) across a desert as part of a brutal "game" or endurance test. The members of the counterculture group, if they reach an American flag at the end of the course, are promised freedom; otherwise, they face imprisonment or death.

## 5. is it possible for a japanese man to enjoy lost in translation

**Score:** 0
**Body:** lost in translation is a fish in water film about two americans who are in japan with lots of cinnamontography that ephasizes how different they are. 

i have never been to japan so it seems dreamy like playing a 90s rpg and i feel a deep longing for the characters bcause of how their faces work and i can tell exactly what they are thinkin (rarely happens in real life)

do u think this movie confuses or offends japanese ppl


confuses = (why do they feel so out of place 2000 inch billboard in 2001 is normal)

offends = (american makes movie about japan and it is not true representation of wha japan is like and almost makes it like a cartoon)


or is japan actually like that?


would they agree with the implication of japanese submission to american cultures and norms (american actor brought in to advertise, constant ppl tryin to speak american but they cant and this is a point of tension (lip vs rip), yet they debase themselves in thse acts at leas tin the service industry)


i am not sayin film is mean or racist to japanese but like can a japanese man enjoy this film

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I mean, maybe.

I know that I, as an American, enjoy foreign movies where people come to America and think it's weird and confusing, Stroszek (1977), Hazard (2005), etc.  There's something really engaging about seeing your culture from the outside.
2. I enjoyed the movie immensely when it came out, but as time went on i realized how much it normalized a certain kind of racism towards Asians like myself.  As if our lives are something that should be twisted into a joke.   Long story short, loved it when i was younger, then came to realize how it affected pop culture in a negative way
3. If they have a sense of humour. I haven't seen Ted Lasso but I get the impression it makes fun of Britain from an American perspective but people like it a lot here because it's funny. It's often interesting to find out what foreigners notice about your culture that you may never have found remarkable. I should think most Japanese people would mostly find it amusing.  It's been a while since I've seen it but 'makes it like a cartoon' sounds unfair. Who can say what a true representation of Japan is?

Also let's not pretend the Japanese haven't portrayed other cultures countless times and not always endearingly.  
  
Also Also - cinnamontography, when is there going to be an academy award category for this?
  - i dont even mean in a is it bad and racist way, i mena more in a person from outside culture tries to portray culture as key point in film. but i wonder how close it was and if it would take them out. i also dont know if the hotels treating americans like gods or the forced assimilation into americna norms as essentially a product is an actual subset of goods. i dont know. i stopped taking caffeine adn stimulnats for 24 hours so ic ant type. 

its like if the mona lisa wa the central plot of a film n the director painted the mona lisa based off ther interpretation. if u never saw the mona lisa in person, u would just assume that it is an honest itnerpetation of the mona lisa, but would ppl who saw the mona lisa already say (this isnt accurate)

i guess it doesnt matter becuase it only serves as the landscape for their dysfunctions and feelings and it isnt about japan, but i was more asking in a quirky way like i wonder if they untinetnionally great film that eveyrbody esxcept japanese ppl could enjoy.
  - >If they have a sense of humour.

It has nothing to do with them having a sense of humor, and everything to do with how offensive the movie may be. I don't think the movie is too offensive (although there's certainly things that point to the casual superiority many white people feel in japan) and I'd also say that many of the things in the movie do touch on the feelings of being an outsider many folks have in Japan. 

>Also let's not pretend the Japanese haven't portrayed other cultures countless times and not always endearingly.

This is irrelevant. Just because Japanese culture has been insensitive to other cultures does not make it right to do the same.

---
